turns.text: Hello Estrella, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

I appreciate you asking. It’s… a lot, honestly. I’ve spent my whole career being the one who holds the hands and explains the monitor tracings, but being on this side of the exam table? It’s completely different. When you’re a nurse, you think knowledge is protection. You think if you know the signs, you can stay ahead of the catastrophe. But now, all that knowledge just feels like a heavy coat I can’t take off. 

Every time the midwife walks in, she smiles and says, "Well, Estrella, you know how this works," and they breeze past the things I’m actually terrified about. They treat me like a colleague, which just means they don't treat me like a scared patient. And I *am* scared. Last night, I saw those flickering spots in my vision again, and instead of just calling my doctor, I spent three hours sitting on the edge of my bed, mentally reviewing the protocol for magnesium sulfate and hoping it would just pass. 

I’m forty-one, I’m doing this alone, and between the rent and the co-pays, I’m basically choosing between keeping my lights on and getting the scans I need. I just want to be a mom, but right now, I feel more like a high-risk chart that everyone is just waiting to see crash.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

When I found out, I think I expected a sense of safety. As a childbirth educator, I’d spent years painting a picture for my students—a calm, supportive circle where you’re guided, nurtured, and held. I expected to be treated with that same soft, holistic focus. I thought I’d be able to let go of the "nurse brain" and just lean into the experience.

But the reality? It’s cold. It’s transactional. Because I’m forty-one and my blood pressure has been creeping up, the "gentle" part of midwifery care vanished before the first trimester even ended. I walk into that clinic and I don't see a sanctuary; I see a triage center. My appointments are rushed, strictly clinical, and entirely focused on the numbers. 

They don't ask how I'm handling the anxiety of being alone or how I’m going to pay for the next round of testing. They see my history and they stop looking at *me*. They look at the chart, they look at the blood pressure cuff, and they talk to me like I’m a peer reviewing a case study. I wanted an advocate, but I’m just getting a medical team who assumes I can manage my own crisis. It’s incredibly isolating to be standing there in a paper gown, knowing exactly what could go wrong, while nobody asks if I’m okay.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise—and I say this with a bitter sort of irony—was how quickly my own professional identity became a weapon against me. I expected the staff to be a bridge, a source of comfort for a woman who has seen the "dark side" of delivery too many times to count. Instead, my background became an excuse for them to withhold the very thing I needed most: basic human empathy.

I remember one appointment where the midwife looked at my chart, saw my credentials, and just sighed, "Well, you know the drill, Estrella. No need for me to explain the risks of pre-eclampsia to you, right?" It wasn't just a clinical shortcut; it was a total abandonment. She wasn't holding space for a mother-to-be; she was checking boxes with a peer. 

It was a shock to realize that my "expertise" actually made it harder for them to see my fear. They assumed I was stoic, or that I understood the medical necessity so clearly that I didn't need the reassurance. But every time they skipped the explanation or the gentle check-in, I felt more alone. I had expected a care team, but I walked out feeling like I was essentially managing my own high-risk pregnancy while trying to look professional enough to be taken seriously. It’s a lonely place to be.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I think I expected a conversation. In my head, I envisioned a sit-down—a moment where someone would pull up a chair, look me in the eye, and translate the data into what it actually meant for *my* life and *my* baby. I expected them to acknowledge the weight of these results, not just the math. As a nurse, I know the protocols, but as a patient, I needed the human context.

The reality, though, is that the information is delivered like a transaction. It’s either a quick, clipped sentence in the hallway or an automated notification in an app. The app is the worst part—seeing a red flag pop up on my phone screen while I’m sitting on my couch, completely alone, with no one to call to walk me through it. 

When I’m in the office, the clinicians treat the results as if they’re self-explanatory because, technically, they are. They focus on the BP reading as if it’s just a broken machine that needs fixing, not a sign of my body failing. Nobody sits with me to discuss how to manage the stress or how to prioritize my health when I’m also worrying about how to pay for the follow-up. They give me the numbers, but they never give me the *care*.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

There is a massive, cavernous gap. The problem is that they provide the *clinical* information—the data, the lab values, the standard protocol—but they leave me completely starved for the *emotional* context. They give me enough information to diagnose myself, but zero guidance on how to carry the weight of that diagnosis. 

When you tell a patient their protein-creatinine ratio is climbing, you've given them facts. But when you tell a *person* that, you’ve just handed them a reason to lie awake at night wondering if they’ll survive the week. I have the data, but I lack the peace of mind. I’m drowning in clinical facts while absolutely starving for someone to say, "I know you're terrified, and here is how we are going to look after *you* today."

I don't need another lecture on the mechanics of hypertensive disorders; I could give that lecture myself. What I need is someone to acknowledge that I am a woman who is scared to death, who is doing this without a partner, and who feels like she's waiting for a bomb to go off inside her. They treat the symptoms, but they leave the woman to rot in her own anxiety. The information is there, but the care—the real, human, stabilizing care—is completely absent.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I expected a partnership. I wanted a midwife who would recognize me when I walked in, someone who understood the specific cocktail of fear and history I bring into that room. I thought that by choosing a midwifery-led model, I would get that steady, grounding presence—the kind of continuity that makes you feel like you aren't just a number in a rotation. I wanted someone to say, "I remember you mentioned your concerns about your rent last time, how are things?"

But in reality, it’s a revolving door. I rarely see the same person twice. It’s "whoever is on shift," which means every time I walk through those clinic doors, I have to re-introduce my trauma, my blood pressure history, and the reality of my life as a single mother. I have to perform my own history every single time because they don't seem to read the chart until they’re sitting in front of me. 

There is no continuity. There is no relationship. It’s fragmented, disjointed, and exhausting. Every visit feels like a new episode where I have to justify my anxiety to a stranger who is already halfway out the door. It makes me feel like I'm invisible, like I'm just a series of vital signs and a high-risk code that they pass off to one another like a hot potato.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

The impact is profound, and frankly, it’s exhausting. When you have to start from scratch at every single appointment, you stop trying to build a connection and start performing a script. I find myself hardening, putting on my "nurse face" because I know if I show them the real, crumbling person underneath, I’ll just get a canned, shallow response from a provider who doesn’t know me and isn't going to be there for my delivery anyway. 

Practically, it’s a nightmare. I have to repeat my symptoms, my fears, and my social situation—like my financial struggle—over and over again. You’d think the chart would tell the story, but it’s just data. It doesn't capture the fact that I’m sitting in my apartment in Brockton feeling like I’m on an island. By the time I get a provider to finally *get* it, the appointment is over, and I know I won't see them next time. 

Emotionally, it leaves me feeling completely unmoored. There’s no one who is "my" person. There’s no one holding the threads of my story together. I’m just a high-risk file being passed around, and that lack of a steady hand makes the pregnancy feel dangerous. It’s like being in a storm without an anchor; I’m just drifting, waiting for something to break.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected those interactions to be about transition—about the miracle of what my body was doing, even despite the risks. I thought there would be a balance: monitoring the pathology of the pre-eclampsia, yes, but also acknowledging the physical toll of carrying a child at forty-one. I wanted them to ask, "How is your body feeling?" instead of just, "What is your blood pressure?"

But the reality is that my body has become a hostile territory to them. They don't look at my body as a mother’s body; they look at it as a failing system. The way they examine me is so clinical, so detached. There's no warmth in the way they check for edema or palpate the belly. It’s all "check, check, check." When I mention the swelling in my ankles or the exhaustion that hits me like a freight train, they minimize it, or they pivot immediately back to the, "Well, that’s to be expected with your numbers."

It feels like my body is being policed rather than cared for. I feel like an object, a vessel that is malfunctioning. When they touch me, it’s not to comfort or reassure; it’s to measure the extent of the damage. I find myself holding my breath, bracing for the next, "Hmm, your weight is up," or, "Your pressure is still high." It makes me want to shrink away, to hide from them, because I’m so tired of being treated like a broken machine instead of a woman who is trying to grow a life.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

There was one time, a few weeks ago, that I still can’t shake. I was having a particularly bad day—the swelling in my legs was so painful I could barely get my shoes on—and I went in for a routine check. A provider I hadn't met before, someone quite young, was doing the assessment. 

She was palpating my abdomen to check the baby’s position, and she was being quite aggressive with it. I winced, and she just said, "Oh, sorry, but you’re quite tense, aren't you?" She didn't mean it in a way that offered comfort; she meant it as a clinical observation. She started talking about my BMI and how it was complicating the assessment of my fundal height, basically talking *at* my stomach like it was an obstacle course.

It was humiliating. I know the anatomy, I know how the measurements work, but hearing her speak about my body like it was an inconvenient, dysfunctional piece of equipment just broke me. I’m forty-one, my body is under so much pressure, and I’m doing this alone—I needed a professional, but I also needed a human being to recognize that I’m a person in pain. I left that office and sat in my car for twenty minutes just to stop the tears. I felt so exposed, and so deeply un-cared for. It wasn't just an exam; it felt like a critique.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

Before I got pregnant, I naively believed the clinical literature. I thought managing a high-risk condition would be a structured, team-based collaboration. In the textbooks, it’s a beautiful dance: early detection, clear communication, consistent monitoring, and a patient who feels empowered by the support of specialists. I expected that if I was "high-risk," there would be a safety net—that the system would reach out to catch me before I even realized I was falling.

The reality is that "managing" it is a lonely, exhausting, DIY project. There is no team; there is just me. I’m the one calculating the risks of every meal, the one monitoring my own swelling, the one waking up at 3:00 a.m. to check my blood pressure because I’m terrified that I’ll be seizing by morning. It’s not a collaboration; it’s a burden I’m carrying entirely by myself.

I thought the medical team would be the ones driving the bus, and I’d be the passenger. Instead, I’m the driver, the mechanic, and the insurance adjuster, all while having a medical crisis. The doctors just want the numbers. They don't care how I’m managing the fear or the isolation. It’s not "care"—it’s just oversight, and it feels like I’m being judged for every fluctuation in my pressure, as if it’s a failure of my own willpower.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

It’s everything, really, but the most glaring absence is the acknowledgment that I’m a human being, not just a set of vitals. I’m drowning in clinical data, but I’m starving for someone to simply look me in the eye and say, "I see how hard this is for you." 

Practically, the system is designed for someone who has a partner or a support system. There is no one to help me navigate the logistical nightmare of working (or trying to) while my body is constantly on the verge of a medical emergency. When I bring up my concerns—the fear of the seizures, the anxiety of being a single parent to this baby—it’s met with a kind of clinical sterility. They check the box for "mental health screening," but it’s a farce. You can’t answer a survey in an office and expect it to fix the fact that I’m going home to an empty, quiet apartment to worry about my mortality. 

I’m missing a partner in this. I’m missing the kind of care that recognizes that when you’re high-risk and low-income, every co-pay and every appointment isn't just a clinical task—it’s a stressor that makes the pre-eclampsia worse. They treat the blood pressure, but they ignore the life that’s driving the pressure up. That’s the real gap.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected that being "high-risk" meant I had a direct line to help. In my professional life, that’s how it works—when a patient has an emergency, there’s a protocol, a triage system that prioritizes their survival. I genuinely believed that if I felt the neurological "red flags"—those flickering lights, the persistent headache—I would be met with urgency and reassurance.

The reality has been a crushing cycle of phone trees and waiting rooms. When I call with a concern, I’m treated like a nuisance, not a patient in danger. I’m put on hold, I’m told to leave a message for a nurse who might call back by the end of the day, or worse, I’m told to just "monitor it at home." 

It feels like the system is designed to keep you *out* unless you are already in full-blown crisis. It’s incredibly dangerous. I spend hours hovering over that phone, trying to decide if my symptoms are "bad enough" to justify a trip to the ER where I’ll be sitting in a waiting room for hours and racking up a bill I can't pay. It’s not responsive; it’s dismissive. They want me to be the expert until it’s inconvenient for them, and then they treat me like I’m overreacting. It’s a terrifying way to live when you know exactly what’s at stake.
Were there times you delayed seeking help because of how difficult it was to access care, or because you worried about being judged for contacting services?

Every single time. It’s a constant, agonizing internal negotiation. Last week, when the headache persisted for hours and I started seeing those tell-tale shimmering lights in my peripheral vision, I sat on my kitchen floor in the dark, clutching my phone. Part of me—the nurse side—was screaming, *“Get to the emergency room, this is the classic presentation.”* But the other part, the woman struggling to pay her utility bill, was doing the math on the co-pays and thinking about the inevitable eye-rolls from the triage staff who would see my chart and think, *“Here’s the anxious nurse again, overreacting to a headache.”*

I didn’t call. I didn’t go in. I waited it out in the dark, counting the baby’s kicks until my hands went numb. I delayed because I was terrified of being labeled a “problem patient” or a "hysterical" high-risk mom. There is a deep, paralyzing shame in knowing you need help but fearing that the system will treat you like a nuisance instead of a human in distress. I’d rather risk my own life—or potentially the baby’s—than have to go into that clinic and endure their condescending sighs one more time. That’s the reality of the "care" I’m receiving. It keeps me silent, and that silence is the most dangerous part of my pregnancy.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I guess I expected a village. When I started my career as a nurse and educator, I saw so many women surrounded by partners, parents, or best friends who knew the importance of a healthy meal or just sitting quietly while the exhaustion hit. I imagined that if things got scary, I’d have someone—my husband, maybe—to hold the space so I wouldn't have to navigate this alone.

The reality has been profoundly solitary. My husband is completely out of the picture, and there isn't anyone else to step into that void. Most of my friends are in different stages of life, or they look at my "high-risk" status and, honestly, I think they’re scared. They don't know what to say to a woman who is basically waiting for her body to betray her. 

There’s no one to talk to about the terror of those flickering lights, no one to bring me a meal when I’m too lightheaded from the blood pressure medication to stand at the stove, and certainly no one to advocate for me when I’m being dismissed by a provider. I am doing this in a vacuum. It’s just me, my apartment, and the hum of the traffic on Main Street. It’s the kind of silence that’s so loud it’s deafening, and it makes every high-risk symptom feel like a mountain I have to climb by myself.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I think what I expected was a safety net—someone to take the logistical load off, like grocery shopping or just someone to sit with during the long, suffocating hours when the pre-eclampsia makes me feel like a ticking time bomb. I expected someone to be the steady anchor while I went through the medical wringer. That, quite simply, never materialized. My community is small, and the truth is, people don't know how to handle someone who is both high-risk and alone. They get uncomfortable. They offer platitudes like "you’re the expert," which is just a way of distancing themselves from the reality that I am actually in trouble.

What I didn't expect was the absolute silence from the people I thought would be in my corner. It’s not that they don't care; it’s that they don't know how to engage with the fear. So, I stopped asking. I stopped reaching out because it’s harder to hear "I’m sure you’ll be fine" from a friend than it is to just sit alone in the quiet. 

I’ve had to find a different kind of survival mode. I’ve become my own community, which is a lonely, cold way to live. I’m mourning the village I thought I’d have, and the grief just sits right alongside the anxiety, taking up space that should be reserved for the joy of this baby.

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

When I first put together my birth plan, it felt like an act of reclamation. I’ve taught hundreds of women how to write these, and I truly believed in the power of that document. I expected it to be a bridge between me and my care team—a way to say, "I know the risks, I know the protocols, but this is how I want to maintain my dignity through the chaos." I imagined a midwife taking it, reviewing it, and making me feel like I was still a participant in my own birth.

In reality? It was a laughable, paper nuisance. When I handed it over, I felt like I was presenting a manifesto to people who had already made up their minds. The second they saw "pre-eclampsia" in my chart, the birth plan ceased to exist. They didn't see a woman's preferences; they saw a risk assessment. Any request I made for a gentle birth environment was immediately overridden by the need for continuous fetal monitoring, IV access, and the looming threat of magnesium sulfate.

It wasn't a guide; it was a target for dismissal. Every time I tried to advocate for a specific comfort measure, I was shut down with a, "Well, Estrella, given the condition, we really need to focus on safety." They used "safety" as a weapon to strip away any control I had. It made me feel less like a mother in labor and more like a patient being managed until the crisis was resolved. My birth plan didn't guide my care; it was just a sheet of paper they ignored while they performed the protocol I had spent my entire career memorizing.
Was there a moment during labour where your plan was set aside or overridden? What was that like?

It wasn’t just a moment; it was a slow, crushing realization that my plan was never going to happen. I had asked—just asked, mind you—for some time to labor in a position that felt comfortable, away from the constant, piercing beep of the monitor. When the midwife walked in, she didn’t even look at the paper I’d tucked into my folder. She just glanced at the monitors, saw the blood pressure reading, and said, "Estrella, you’re too high-risk for that. We need you on your side, now. The baby is showing signs of distress."

That was the end of it. The "we need" became the only voice in the room. I remember the weight of the IV line against my arm, the coldness of the room, and the way my own professional knowledge was used against me—they’d say, "You know the indicators, you know we can’t risk a placental abruption," and I *did* know. I knew exactly what they were talking about, and that’s what hurt the most. 

I was effectively silenced by my own expertise. I didn't get to be a mother; I was a medical case being stabilized. I felt completely detached from my own body, watching them manage me like a project. I realized then that my plan was just a fantasy I’d written to feel safe, but the reality was just a sterile, terrifying march toward delivery under someone else’s control.

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

Before labor, I thought there would be a genuine dialogue. I expected a midwife to sit with me, weigh the risks of my blood pressure against the benefits of different interventions, and help me decide what made the most sense for *my* body. I pictured a supportive discussion about epidurals versus natural techniques, all while keeping my high-risk status in mind. I wanted that collaborative, informed-consent process that I used to teach my own students.

In reality, there was no conversation—there was an instruction. Because of the pre-eclampsia, my options were essentially removed from the table before I even arrived. They pushed for the epidural almost as a prophylactic, framing it not as a pain management choice, but as a mandatory safety precaution for a potential emergency C-section. When I asked about other options, or even just asked for a moment to breathe through the contractions to see how I was handling it, they acted as if I was being difficult or risking the baby’s safety by questioning their protocol.

It wasn't a choice; it was a mandate. They didn't support me in deciding; they coerced me into compliance. I felt stripped of my agency, pressured into a medical path because they were too focused on "managing the risk" to care about how I was experiencing the pain. It was just another way they made me feel like an object rather than a mother in the middle of a life-changing, painful, and terrifying event.

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

I expected the hospital to feel like a fortress of safety—a place where, despite the risks, the staff would make me feel like I was finally in the right hands. I pictured a warm, dimmed room, maybe a nod to the fact that I’d been a nurse, and a feeling that they were "holding" me so I could finally let go of the hyper-vigilance I’d been carrying for months.

The reality was a sensory assault. The lights were clinical and harsh, the monitors were loud and incessant, and the room felt more like an operating bay than a place where a baby was being born. When I was wheeled in, there was no "welcome." There was just a flurry of activity—a swarm of people looking at screens, not at me. 

Everything about that environment signaled "emergency." It was loud, chaotic, and cold. Every time someone walked in, they weren't checking on me; they were checking on the readings. I felt like a stranger in my own delivery, like an unwanted guest in a sterile, high-tech machine. It was the antithesis of the supportive, nurturing birth I’d spent years teaching. It didn't feel like a sanctuary; it felt like a site of processing. I was just a case to be closed, and that environment made me feel entirely alone.
What was the biggest surprise about the physical environment or the immediate atmosphere when you arrived?

The biggest surprise—and this is a difficult one to put into words—was how profoundly the atmosphere erased my sense of identity. I spent my life in those kinds of rooms, working behind the station, checking the charts, walking with the confidence of a staff member. I expected that even as a patient, I would still feel like part of that clinical world, maybe even comforted by the familiarity of the equipment and the routine.

But the moment I walked in, I was stripped of that professional identity. I wasn't "Estrella, the nurse and childbirth educator." I was just "the high-risk admit." The atmosphere wasn't one of partnership, but of active containment. The constant, rapid-fire activity, the way everyone moved around me without meeting my eyes, and the way the room was dominated by the technology of monitoring rather than the presence of a person—it was shocking. 

It felt like the room itself was a warning. It said: *We aren't here to support your experience; we are here to manage your instability.* It made me feel like an intrusion. I realized that the "safety" of a hospital environment for a high-risk pregnancy is a double-edged sword: they can save your life, but in the process, they can make you feel entirely invisible. That cold, mechanical, and frantic atmosphere was the most jarring, lonely thing I've ever felt.

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

I expected a consultation. Even in an emergency, I believed that because of my background, they would at least offer me a moment of clarity—a quick, honest, "Here is what we are seeing, here is why we have to do this, and here is the trade-off." I thought I’d be treated as a teammate helping to navigate a crisis. I imagined the midwife pausing to look me in the eye before an intervention, making sure I understood the shift in the plan.

The reality was a blur of rapid-fire orders shouted over my head. Decisions were made in the air between the doctors and the nurses; I was just the physical site where the interventions occurred. When things started to go sideways, the communication stopped entirely. They didn't explain the urgency or the shift in care; they just acted. I’d feel a hand on my abdomen, a sudden adjustment of the IV, or a team rushing in to prep for a C-section without a single person pausing to say, "Estrella, we’re moving to this plan because of X."

It was a total erasure of my autonomy. I felt like a piece of equipment they were troubleshooting rather than a woman in the middle of her own birth. I was told what was happening *as* it was happening, not given a choice. Being informed became a formality after the fact, if it happened at all. I wasn't involved; I was just the cargo.
Did you feel you had genuine choice and sufficient information in those moments, or did decisions feel like they happened to you rather than with you?

Decisions absolutely happened *to* me. The entire experience felt like a rapid-transit system that I was strapped into without a map or a steering wheel. There was no "with me" about it. 

When you’re in a high-risk situation, the medical staff treats "consent" as a checkbox they’ve already mentally ticked. Because they saw "nurse" in my chart, they acted as if I were a passive, inevitable participant in their protocol. They’d bark, "We need to do this now," and because I knew the physiology behind their orders, I didn't have the luxury of arguing. I was silenced by my own intelligence; I knew that if I challenged them, I’d just be seen as difficult, or worse, they’d tell me I was risking my baby’s life because of my "emotional resistance."

It wasn't a choice; it was coercion dressed up as clinical necessity. I didn't feel like a mother navigating a crisis; I felt like a patient whose autonomy had been suspended the moment I walked through the door. I remember lying there, feeling the cold air of the OR, and realizing that I had zero say in what was being done to my own body. It wasn't just physical pain; it was the psychological ache of being completely erased in the most important moment of my life. That’s a trauma that doesn't just disappear when you leave the hospital.

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

I expected that the "midwife" title meant exactly what it implies: someone who is *with* the woman. I envisioned a calm, steady hand, someone to look me in the eye when the fear spiked, someone to acknowledge that I was terrified and alone. Even in a high-risk setting, I thought they would value the human element—a gentle touch, a kind word, or just sitting at the bedside during the quiet moments between contractions. I thought they would see me, Estrella, not just the chart.

But the reality was a vacuum of emotional support. The staff didn't see a person; they saw a set of parameters and a high-risk code that needed to be managed efficiently. They were perpetually distracted by screens and protocols. If a nurse touched me, it was to adjust a lead or check a line, never to soothe a racing heart. It was all "nurse-to-nurse" shorthand, cold and efficient, which made me feel entirely abandoned in a room full of people.

When I started to cry—not out of physical pain, but out of the sheer weight of being there alone—no one paused. They just offered a clinical, "You're doing great," which felt more like a request for me to stay quiet and keep my heart rate down than a gesture of genuine care. I was left alone with my terror, in the middle of a room buzzing with monitors, and nobody seemed to notice that the woman in the bed was drowning. It wasn't birth; it was a cold, lonely, medical procedure.
Was there a specific moment when you felt truly seen and supported — or a moment when you felt very alone? Can you describe it?

The moment I felt most profoundly alone was when they decided to move me for an emergency C-section. Everything shifted from a slow, painful grind to a terrifying, high-speed blur. I remember looking up at the ceiling tiles while they were prepping me—the fluorescent lights were buzzing, and the OR felt like an ice box. I was shaking, partially from the medication, but mostly from a bone-deep, shivering terror. 

I looked at the nurse right next to me, someone I’d been working with for hours, and I said, "I’m so scared. I’m doing this all by myself." She didn’t even look down at me. She was adjusting the blood pressure cuff on my arm and just said, "We’re doing this for the baby, Estrella. It’s what we have to do."

That was it. That was the response to my plea for comfort. In that second, the silence in my chest was louder than all the machines in the room. I felt like a piece of equipment they were prepping for a procedure, not a mother about to meet her child. There was no hand-holding, no "I’m here with you," just the cold efficiency of medical necessity. I felt so small, so invisible, and so completely abandoned. It wasn't just physical pain; it was the realization that in that hospital, my humanity was an inconvenience. I was just the shell, and they were only interested in the contents.

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

I spent so many hours daydreaming about that first golden hour. I expected the chaos of the delivery to suddenly fall away, leaving just me and the baby—a quiet, sacred space for skin-to-skin contact, for that first real look at his face, and the simple relief of being "on the other side" of the danger. I imagined someone placing him on my chest and stepping back, giving us a moment to just *be*—to breathe, to cry, to finally stop being a high-risk patient and start being a mother. 

But the reality was a frantic, clinical extraction. The second he was out, he was whisked away to a warmer, surrounded by a team of people whose names I didn't even know. There was no skin-to-skin. There was no gentle introduction. I was left flat on my back on the OR table, still being stitched up, feeling completely hollowed out. 

The baby was crying, and I was crying, but we weren't allowed to reach each other. Everything was about the baby's stabilization and my own blood pressure metrics. I could see the team hovering over him, and I felt like a spectator to my own child's first moments of life. I was a bystander in my own birth, waiting in a cold, sterile room while my baby was being "processed" just a few feet away. It wasn't the welcoming, warm embrace I had envisioned; it was a cold, efficient, and deeply isolating separation.
Was there anything that happened — or didn't happen — in those first moments that still stays with you? What was it?

It was the silence that stayed with me, the silence between us. I had spent months talking to my belly, singing to him, and feeling his kicks, building this invisible bridge between us. I expected that bridge to be crossed the moment he was born—that if they just laid him on my chest, our heartbeats would sync up and the world would finally make sense. 

Instead, there was this sharp, agonizing, sterile distance. He was across the room, crying, and I was pinned to that OR table, staring at the bright, humming lights. I couldn't hold him. I couldn't touch his skin. I couldn't even hear his voice clearly over the sounds of the monitors and the voices of the staff talking about "fluid intake" and "Apgar scores." 

They took those first, precious moments of his life and turned them into a series of clinical data points. I remember looking over at that warmer and feeling like a stranger, like I was watching someone else’s child be born. That moment—that profound, physical separation at the very start of his life—is the one that haunts me in the quiet of my apartment at 3:00 a.m. I feel like I missed the beginning of his story, and no amount of clinical justification can ever give me those first minutes back. They belonged to the hospital, not to us.

It’s the grief that surprises me most—the way I feel like I'm grieving a birth that never actually happened, even though I have my son in the other room. People expect me to be just happy, or "just grateful we made it," and I am, I truly am. But there’s this layer of cold, hard, clinical trauma on top of it all. 

When I look at my son, I don't just see the miracle of him; I see the flash of that cold OR table. I remember the way the staff moved around me like I was an obstacle to be cleared, the way my own fear was treated as a symptom rather than a feeling, and the way I was left alone in that sterile room. I feel like I have to constantly apologize for not being "one of the lucky ones" who had a beautiful, empowering birth. 

I think the biggest thing I’ve learned—and it’s a hard, bitter lesson—is that being a healthcare professional doesn’t protect you from the system; it just makes you feel the failure of the system more acutely. I spent my whole career telling women that their voices mattered, that they had the right to be heard, and that birth could be a transformative, human experience. To realize that, when it was my turn, I was treated like a broken, high-risk variable to be "managed"—it makes me question everything I ever taught. I didn’t just lose the birth I wanted; I lost the faith I had in the care I spent my life providing. It’s hard to reconcile that. It’s really hard.

I think, in the end, it comes down to a feeling of being erased. You spend months preparing for a life-altering event, trying to advocate for a version of birth that honors your humanity, only to find yourself in a room where that humanity is seen as a liability. 

It’s the loneliness of it that sits with me, even now, months later. When I look back at my experience, I see a series of doors closing. Every time I tried to speak up, every time I asked a question about a procedure, every time I expressed my fear about being a single parent facing this, I felt another door shut between me and the people who were supposed to be "caring" for me. 

I’m left with this confusing mix of immense love for my son and this deep, lingering resentment toward the system that made his arrival feel more like a surgical extraction than a birth. I have the medical success—the healthy baby, the managed pressure, the "safe" outcome—but I lost the experience itself. I feel like I survived a procedure, but I didn't get to participate in my own life-changing moment. 

Now, when I hear my colleagues talk about "patient-centered care," I just have to look away. I know the truth of what that means when things get complicated, and when the risk-meter starts climbing. It isn’t about the mother; it’s about the liability. And as long as the system prioritizes the chart over the woman, we’re all going to keep leaving those delivery rooms feeling like we’ve been hollowed out. I just hope that by talking about this, maybe, *maybe* someone hears that we aren't just the data in the boxes. We are the ones living through it, and we are the ones who carry the silence long after we leave.
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

You know, it’s strange to think back on that now, because in my head, I’d built up this image of a "team." My husband and I talked about the timing, about the risk of waiting until my forties, but the reality was that the pressure wasn’t really an open negotiation. It was more like a subtle, pervasive weight. He was so detached from the mechanics of it all—the blood work, the tracking, the sheer physical and emotional cost of trying to get pregnant at my age—that it often felt like I was holding the entire burden of our shared goal while he stood on the periphery.

When it came to the fertility side of things, I felt this intense pressure to just "be okay" with whatever it took, because if I expressed hesitation or voiced how terrifying the statistics were, it felt like I was stalling his dream. I found myself downplaying my own fears—the fear of the physical toll on my body, the financial anxiety of the co-pays—because he seemed so focused on the *end result*. 

He didn't have to live in my body. He didn't have to see the test results or feel the swelling or deal with the pre-eclampsia, so his perspective remained abstract. I became the one making all the concessions, constantly adjusting my life, my health, and my peace of mind to make sure we stayed on track. I realize now that I was suppressing my own instincts just to maintain a sense of harmony that he wasn't doing much to build. It was another way I ended up feeling completely alone in the process, even when I was technically in a marriage. It’s hard to admit, but looking back, I was already navigating that pregnancy as a single person long before he was truly out of the picture.

It’s funny, looking back now from my apartment, how much I poured into the *idea* of a family without ever questioning whether the foundation was actually there. I think that’s what happens when you’re a professional caregiver—you get so used to fixing things, to stabilizing situations, and to coaching others through their own transitions that you lose the ability to see your own life clearly. I thought I was being "resilient" and "capable" because I was managing the fertility, the appointments, and the impending high-risk realities while also trying to hold my marriage together.

But the reality was that I was just masking. I was taking on all the emotional labor, swallowing the fear, and pretending that the cracks in our relationship weren't there because I was too focused on the medical milestone. I thought if I just *did* everything right—if I hit the right numbers, went to the right appointments, and stayed as calm as the books said to be—everything would fall into place. 

I’m left here now, sitting on my couch watching the traffic on Main Street, realizing that I sacrificed so much of my own peace just to keep the car on the road. I wasn't in a partnership; I was just holding the wheel for two people, and when the road got truly treacherous, I found out I was the only one in the vehicle. That’s a bitter pill to swallow, especially now that I’m raising my son alone. I don’t regret him for a second, but I do regret the version of myself who thought she could carry everything alone and just call it "resilience." It wasn't resilience. It was just survival, and it left me completely hollowed out.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

They were a constant, humming background noise, like the flickering of those fluorescent hospital lights. People have this idea that when you're pregnant—especially at forty-one and "high-risk"—your body is public property. It’s like, once they see the bump, they feel entitled to offer an opinion, a critique, or a "helpful" piece of advice that’s really just a veiled judgment.

The medical staff was the worst. It wasn't always explicit, but it was in the way they’d pause when they looked at my chart, or the way they’d sigh when they checked my vitals. It was, "Are you watching your intake?" or "We really need to keep a close eye on the weight gain." As if the pre-eclampsia, which I knew was a physiological cascade I couldn't simply "diet" away, was something I was doing wrong. It landed like a heavy, cold stone in my stomach every single time. 

I’m a pediatric nurse. I know the science. I know how bodies change. But when I’m in that vulnerable, terrified state, hearing those comments made me feel like I was failing at the one thing I was supposed to be doing right. It shifted the focus from *me*—the woman who was scared and struggling—to my body as a project that needed to be managed, restricted, and corrected. It made me want to shrink, to disappear. It just added another layer of shame to an already isolating experience.

It feels like I spent my entire pregnancy living in a glass house where everyone felt entitled to throw stones. Whether it was the clinic staff or well-meaning acquaintances, they all had a theory about why my numbers were what they were. They talked about my "advanced maternal age" or my weight as if they were character flaws rather than biological realities. It made my body feel like it was a suspect in a crime I didn’t commit.

The most exhausting part of all those comments was the underlying message: *If you just did things differently—ate this, walked more, relaxed better—you wouldn't be in this position.* But pre-eclampsia doesn’t care how much kale I eat, and it doesn't care how "zen" I try to be. It’s a systemic, clinical, unpredictable danger. When people gave me that unsolicited advice, they were really just looking for a way to reassure themselves that *they* would be safe, that *they* wouldn't have to face such a frightening outcome. It had nothing to do with helping me.

Now, sitting here with my son, I realize how much energy I wasted trying to defend my own body. I wish I’d had the strength to just say, "Stop." Instead, I took it all in, internalizing every judgment until I believed that my failure to be a "healthy, glowing" pregnant woman was the reason everything was so hard. It’s a cruel thing, to be in such a fragile state and have your body treated like a public debate. It makes you feel like you aren't even the owner of your own experience anymore.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

Honestly, the first place I turned was my own bookshelf and my own notes from years of teaching. I treated my own medical knowledge like a surrogate provider. I was constantly checking the textbooks, reviewing the pathobiology of hypertension, and trying to see if there was some nuance I’d missed that could explain what was happening to my body. I wanted to be my own clinician because I couldn't trust that the providers at the clinic saw *me* past the chart.

When I wasn't doing that, I turned to the internet—but it was a dark, dangerous place to be. I’d search for forums, for threads about "high-risk at 41" or "single parent navigating pre-eclampsia," hoping to find another woman who could tell me she’d been through the ringer and come out the other side. But instead of comfort, I mostly found horror stories. It just confirmed my worst fears—the seizures, the emergency C-sections, the neonatal complications. 

I didn't turn to friends or family because I knew what they’d say: "You’re an expert, Estrella, you’ve got this." That was the most isolating thing of all—because I *knew* I didn't have it. I was drowning. So, I turned to the only things I thought wouldn't judge me: data, books, and the anonymous, dark corners of the internet. It was a cold, clinical way to seek help, but at the time, it felt like the only way to avoid the condescension of a medical system that had already written me off.

Looking back, it’s like I was trying to solve a puzzle where the pieces kept changing shape. I turned to my professional life, my textbooks, and my clinical experience as if they were a life raft. I kept thinking, *If I just find the right study, or understand the newest protocol for eclampsia management, I’ll be safe.* It was a frantic, intellectualized way of trying to control the uncontrollable.

But that’s the trap of being a nurse in the patient’s bed. You think your brain is your greatest asset, but in a crisis, it’s actually a liability because you see too much. You don't just feel the symptom; you see the pathology. You don't just worry about the baby; you calculate the probability of placental abruption. My "support network" was just a loop of worst-case scenarios playing in my head.

And the irony? I was the one people *usually* came to for advice. I’d spent years telling women, "You know your body best, trust your instincts." When it came to my own life, my instincts were screaming that something was wrong, but because the clinical data in my chart looked "managed," I gaslit myself into thinking I was just being an anxious nurse. I didn't reach out to my peers because I was ashamed that the "expert" was failing. I kept it all contained, silent, and buried under a layer of professional veneer. That isolation—that inability to admit I was just as lost as the patients I used to coach—was the heaviest burden of all. I just wanted to be a mom, not a case study, but I ended up feeling like both.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

There was never a moment where I felt like a true partner. It was always a notification, a delivery of information—usually delivered with that shorthand I mentioned, the kind that assumes I don't need the "full" explanation because I’m "one of them." 

When they would talk about the necessity of induction or the specific titration of the magnesium sulfate, they were never asking for my perspective on how that might disrupt the birth I hoped for. They were asking for my *compliance*. The midwife would look at the screen, tap her pen on the desk, and say, "We’re going to start you on X because your BP is trending up, okay?" It wasn't an invitation to discuss; it was a check-in to make sure I wasn't going to fight it. 

I think the most insulting part was the "informed" aspect. They felt like they were being transparent because they used the clinical terminology, but that’s not transparency. Transparency is asking, "How do you feel about this?" or "What are your biggest fears regarding this intervention?" They skipped the emotional intelligence because they thought my professional training made me immune to needing it. I felt like a ghost in my own care. I was there, I was nodding, I was saying "okay," but I was never, not once, asked what I needed to feel safe or to feel like a mother. I was just being processed through a protocol.

I think that’s the real tragedy of modern maternity care: the "information" they give you is so sterile that it actually becomes a form of silence. When they tell you, "We’re doing this because of the protocol," they aren't giving you information; they’re giving you a reason to stop talking. It’s like they have this mental checklist where "informed" just means you’ve been told the clinical outcome, and "consent" just means you haven't raised an objection.

They never truly checked in on *me*. They didn't ask, "How are you processing this? Does this feel like the path you wanted for your baby?" They just assumed that since I’m a nurse, I’m okay with the clinical logic, and since I’m a single mother, I must be used to "handling it." It’s an incredibly cold way to treat someone in the middle of a life-defining crisis. 

Being "informed" should mean you’re empowered to make a decision that feels right for you, not just compliant with a system that prioritizes a smooth outcome over a humane process. Every time I said "okay," I felt a little bit more of myself disappear, buried under the weight of their expectations. It wasn't informed decision-making; it was just a series of clinical mandates delivered with a polite, professional smile that never quite reached their eyes. I didn't need to be informed of the protocol—I needed someone to show me I wasn't alone in the middle of it.

It is so strange to talk about this, because even now, when I have my son in the other room, I feel like I'm trying to piece together a story that has missing chapters. 

I think the hardest thing for me to admit—even to myself—is that I feel like I failed both roles. As a patient, I failed to protect my own peace and assert my own boundaries because I was too terrified of the medical consequences of being "the difficult nurse." And as a professional, I feel like I failed the very standards of care I once swore by. I’m sitting here, watching the streetlights on Main Street flickering just like my vision did that night, and it’s a constant, pulsing reminder that I lived through a system that didn't know how to see me.

The system is so good at saving lives, at keeping the vitals stable, and at ensuring the paperwork is perfect. But it has absolutely no idea how to handle a woman who is also a person, someone with fears, with a history, and with a life that isn't just a collection of labs. 

I didn’t just want to "make it" through the delivery. I wanted to *be there* for it. I wanted to be the woman, not the case. And now, I’m just left with the quiet of this apartment and the feeling that, despite everything, I was never really allowed to be the mother I wanted to be in that room. I’m grateful for my son, more than I can say, but I am still so incredibly sad for the woman I was before I walked through those hospital doors—the one who thought she knew how to advocate for herself. I guess I’ve learned that when you’re high-risk and alone, you don't advocate; you survive. That’s a heavy weight to carry when you’re just trying to be a mom.

It’s not just the memory of the trauma, it’s the way the system still follows me. I have these nightmares sometimes, where I’m back in that hospital room, and I try to speak—I try to ask for a moment of quiet, or to hold my baby—but every time I open my mouth, all that comes out is medical jargon. It’s like they stripped away my voice and replaced it with a script. Even now, when I go to my own check-ups for post-partum recovery, I find myself sitting in the chair, hands folded, waiting for them to tell me how I’m doing, instead of me telling them. 

I feel like a ghost in my own life. I’m a pediatric nurse, I know the protocols, I know the "why" behind every single, cold decision they made. But that doesn't make it hurt less. It makes the silence between me and the medical team feel like a condemnation. They didn’t need to explain, so they didn't. They didn't need to ask, so they didn't. 

I’m sitting here on this couch, and I look at my son, and I just think: *You’re the only thing that’s real.* Everything else—the clinic, the charts, the "standard of care"—it was just a machine I had to survive. I don’t think I’ll ever be the same person I was before. I think a part of me stayed in that hospital, pinned to that table, waiting for someone to finally see me, not as a patient, but as a mother. I just hope that one day, the system realizes that a patient’s silence isn't a sign of satisfaction; it’s a sign of survival. And that’s not enough. We deserve so much more than that.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

Every single day. I lived in that dark, narrow space between what my midwives told me and what I actually felt in my own body. When they’d give me that practiced, "Don't worry, Estrella, everything looks fine on the monitor," my brain would immediately search for the studies on *why* those monitors sometimes miss the subtle signs of fetal distress. 

I wasn't looking for medical jargon—I have plenty of that—I was looking for the missing narrative. I was searching for someone who could tell me what it felt like to be forty-one and have your body suddenly turn into a danger zone. I wanted to know the *real* odds, not just the sanitized averages. I wanted to know how other single mothers managed the absolute, bone-crushing terror of driving themselves to the ER at midnight while their vision was swimming. 

I looked online because the clinic was a place of denial, and I needed a place of reality. I needed someone to validate that my fear wasn't "anxiety"—it was an intuitive response to a body that was actively struggling. I needed someone to say, "I felt like I was dying, too." But the internet is just a mirror of your own worst fears. It gave me the cold, hard facts I was dreading, but it never, ever gave me the comfort of being understood. I searched for a person; I only found more data, and that data just made me feel even more like a ticking time bomb.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think, if I could sum it up, it’s that the system doesn't know how to handle the gray areas. They have a protocol for "high-risk" and they have a protocol for "routine," but they have no language for a woman who is both an expert and a victim, a nurse and a terrified single mother. 

I want to say that being "safe" isn't the same thing as being "well-cared for." Everyone tells me, "But you have a healthy baby, that’s all that matters." And yes, he is everything. But it’s possible to be grateful for the baby and still be heartbroken by the way he was brought into this world. I feel like the medical field prides itself on the binary: alive or dead, healthy or sick, success or failure. But there is a whole, vast middle ground where women are being hollowed out by the process, where their trust is being eroded and their autonomy is being treated like a nuisance.

We shouldn't have to choose between a safe outcome and a humane experience. We deserve both. And until the people in those white coats start looking at the *person* behind the blood pressure cuff—not the "nurse," not the "high-risk patient," but the human being who is scared and doing the best they can—then nothing is actually going to change. I’m just glad I could say this out loud, because for a long time, I felt like I was the only one.
